title: Glory and Singing to the Ends of the Earth
speaker: Pastor Casey Southerland
source_files: 8-4-24 Glory and Singing to the Ends of the Earth - GUEST SPEAKER - Pastor Casey Southerland - Ps 96.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Hardwired to Admire Greatness
subpoints: We admire amazing feats — athletes who perform under immense pressure (Olympics illustration)
When we recognize something is great, we admire it, talk about it, sing its praises
Psalm 96 calls us to recognize the greatness of the Lord and sing his praises to the ends of the earth

outline.title: Sing to the Lord — Verses 1-3
subpoints: Sing, sing, sing — the Hebrew superlative form echoes "Holy, holy, holy"
The song is directed to the Lord (Yahweh — all caps)
The theme is his salvation, his glory, his marvelous work — the cross of Christ
The scope: all the earth — already in the Old Testament, a vision broader than Israel alone
The duty: tell of his salvation day to day — speak well of God, share his goodness with neighbors
Declare his glory among the nations — this is the Great Commission in Psalm 96

outline.title: Why He Deserves the Praise — Verses 4-6
subpoints: For great is the Lord and greatly to be praised
He is to be feared above all gods — all the gods of the peoples are worthless idols
The Lord made the heavens — splendor and majesty are before him
Revelation 7:9-12: the great multitude from every nation, crying 'Salvation belongs to our God'
Even angels who have never sinned worship him — how much more should redeemed sinners

outline.title: Give God What He Deserves — Verses 7-9
subpoints: Ascribe to the Lord, O families of the peoples, ascribe to the Lord glory and strength
Bring an offering and come into his courts
Worship the Lord in the splendor of holiness — tremble before him, all the earth
True worship shakes us — we tremble in the presence of the God to whom we will give account

outline.title: The Lord Reigns and His Judgment Brings Joy — Verses 10-13
subpoints: "Say among the nations: the Lord reigns!" — a message to take
Righteous judgment is a good thing: contrast between oppressive human rulers (Haiti illustration; Micah's day) and the Lord's equity
Micah 5:2-4: the promise of a ruler from Bethlehem who will be great to the ends of the earth
The creation itself rejoices at the coming judgment: heavens, earth, sea, fields, trees
He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples in his faithfulness

outline.title: Conclusion: A Call to Go
subpoints: It is a question of our devotion — we are called to go and say among the nations, "The Lord reigns"
We are called to declare his glory — there is action for us to take
Can you share one thing where God's mercy has been demonstrated to you? Be ready to testify
When someone asks how you are, bring the glory of God into your answer

summary: Preached by guest speaker Pastor Casey Southerland during the Sunday morning service, this sermon expounds Psalm 96 as a vision of God's glory being declared to the ends of the earth. The sermon opens with an extended illustration from the Olympics — humanity is hardwired to admire greatness, and when we see it, we naturally sing its praises. Psalm 96 calls God's people to recognize the supreme greatness of the Lord and to carry that praise to every nation. The sermon works through the Psalm in four movements: the call to sing to the Lord and tell of his salvation day to day (vv. 1-3); the reason he deserves the praise — he alone made the heavens, and every idol is worthless (vv. 4-6); the command to ascribe glory to him and worship him in holy trembling (vv. 7-9); and the proclamation that the Lord reigns and his coming judgment is a cause for joy, not fear (vv. 10-13). The sermon draws on Revelation 7 for a vision of the eschatological worship of the Lamb, and on Micah 5:2-4 to show how the promise of a ruler from Bethlehem who will be "great to the ends of the earth" is the OT backdrop of this missionary vision. The sermon closes with a practical challenge: every believer should be ready to share one thing where God's mercy was demonstrated to them, and be intentional about speaking of his glory in everyday conversation.
transcript:
## Introduction: Hardwired to Admire Greatness

Please keep your Bibles open there to Psalm 96, and we will come before the Lord's Word. What I hope to do this morning is to lay before you in a fresh way a vision for the glory of the Lord to the ends of the earth. That is what the Psalmist here, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, has in view. And that is what we are called to as God's people.

Ask you to bow your heads one more time as we approach the Lord in His Word. Gracious Father, we come to you and we thank you for the revelation of your Word — that you do not send us out to wonder and to wander to find your will, but you have laid it out before us. You are a kind and gracious God who leads us along the way, leading us by still waters, nourishing our souls. I pray you would use this morning, by the preaching and the faithful hearing of your Word. In Christ's name. Amen.

So we all, I believe, are aware that the Olympics are going on right now. Maybe the Olympia Church is not aware that you have been working so hard, but that is going on while we have been here. It is hard to imagine now, but there was a time when I thought of myself as an athlete, and I loved things like the Olympics. I was a highlight junkie. I would find those things that caused the imagination to soar at what people — ordinary people, but people — could do.

When I was first — this was before my time, but the first thing I remember hearing of related to the Olympics was Jesse Owens being dominant in Germany. And then Carl Lewis, the sprinter and long jumper, had ten medals and nine of them gold. Then we think of things like the speed of Usain Bolt. I remember Nadia Comaneci — that young lady, the first gymnast to receive a perfect ten as a score. And one that is near and dear to the American Olympic fan is the name Kerri Strug. If you remember, watching as Team USA had never before won a gold medal, and on the last opportunity, with an ankle that was badly injured — she had torn two ligaments — she delivered a vault that earned the gold, the very first gold for the women's team.

And it's not only the physical feats that I find impressive when I think about the Olympics, but it's also the ability to perform at such a high level in that very moment when it has to happen. These athletes, under immense pressure, at the culmination of a lifetime of commitment, before the scrutinizing eyes of the world — to put it all together and perform at the highest level. It's just an amazing thing.

I think our hearts are hardwired to admire amazing things. We admire greatness when we see it. When we recognize something is great, we admire it. We talk about it. We sing its praises — literally. If there's someone who's done something impressive and you go along and talk about that to someone else, it's called "singing their praises." And in Psalm 96, we are called to sing the praises of the Lord God.

## Sing to the Lord — Verses 1-3

So Psalm 96 calls us to recognize the greatness of the Lord, his glory, and to sing his praises to the ends of the earth. As we walk down through this short Psalm, I'd like you to consider a few things.

The first things are in verses 1-3. We are called to sing to the Lord: "Sing to the Lord a new song. Sing to the Lord, all the earth. Sing to the Lord, bless his name." You hear that — sing, sing, sing. In Hebrew, we see the superlative expressed this way: holy, holy, holy. And now we have sing, sing, sing. And these things echo in the hearts and minds of the hearers.

And it is a song that is to go to the Lord. It's the thrice-holy God receiving singing and praise in the superlative. And what is the theme of the song? His salvation, his glory, his marvelous work. Nothing shines more glorious than the work of the Lord Jesus Christ on the cross. While we were sinners — while we were sinners — he died for us.

So the singing has a particular object. It's not just singing the praises of great things we see among men. It's singing the praises of the Lord God — you'll notice it's in all caps there, and that's the clue that it's the word Yahweh, speaking of Jehovah, the one who released his people, redeemed them from slavery, brought them out, became their God and they his people.

And who should be singing? Look at our first three verses: "Sing to the Lord a new song. Sing to the Lord, all the earth." I thought the Jews were the chosen people, this special people, but do you see here embedded in the Old Testament this vision that is much broader than the nation of Israel alone — a vision for the glory of God in all the earth, all of the earth?

So all peoples and all places are called to sing to the Lord. "Bless his name, tell of his salvation from day to day." Brothers and sisters, you who know Christ, who have him as your Savior — do you and do I speak of his salvation day to day? Do people hear us singing his praises? Not literally only, but in a figurative way — do they hear us speaking of the goodness of God and his mercy in our lives, his upholding us through difficult times? And when difficulties do come our way, can they see in you that you know God to be trustworthy, that you know God to be faithful? Do you bless his name and tell of his salvation — what he's done in your life to unshackle you from sin?

"Declare his glory" — verse 3. Now this declaring of his glory implies that there's a group that already understands the glory of God. And so that's the Great Commission here in Psalm 96: go, go into all the world, go and speak — you are to be his witnesses, because you are the ones who with conviction and experience can speak of his marvelous works in your life.

I'll give you one point of clear application: can you think of one thing, one significant thing in your life, where God's mercy has been demonstrated to you — something you recognize you could not have managed without him? Could you be ready to share that? And when someone says, "How are you doing?" can you find a way to work the glory of God into it? Many people say, "Better than I deserve." I think that's excellent. It speaks of God's mercy and grace and gives opportunity to go and explain more as the Lord allows.

## Why He Deserves the Praise — Verses 4-6

Next we see in verses 4-6 the reason why he deserves the praise. Verse 4: "For great is the Lord. He is to be feared above all gods. For all the gods of the peoples are worthless idols, but the Lord made the heavens." So everything else that man puts ahead of him — all these idols, all the false gods, all the idols of our hearts — are worthless. They fall short of the satisfaction that we ultimately entirely desire. And splendor and majesty are before him.

You remember what the Apostle John expressed in Revelation 7? He said, "After this I looked and behold, a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb, clothed in white robes with palm branches in their hands, and crying out with a loud voice: 'Salvation belongs to our God who sits on the throne and to the Lamb.'" These gathered at the throne are singing of his salvation, just as you and I are called to do by Psalm 96.

And then it continues: all the angels were standing around the throne and they fell on their faces before the throne and worshiped God, saying, "Amen! Blessing and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honor and power and might be to our God forever and ever. Amen!" Those angels — who had not rebelled, who had not been redeemed from their sin, whose sins were not nailed to the cross — those angels standing around the throne also gave praise. So those of us who have been fallen and in desperate need of the saving grace of the Lord Jesus Christ have everything to sing for.

Consider this, brothers and sisters: his glory is immense. His glory goes beyond your salvation. Even if you had never sinned, even if there were no sin, God would still be worthy of all our praise.

## Give God What He Deserves — Verses 7-9

Next we see a command to give God what he deserves, verses 7 and following: "Ascribe to the Lord, O families of the peoples, ascribe to the Lord glory and strength. Ascribe to the Lord the glory due his name. Bring an offering and come into his courts." Three times — give to the Lord, ascribe to the Lord. This is what the Lord deserves, and we are to give it to him.

"Worship the Lord in the splendor of holiness. Tremble before him, all the earth." This is what God calls us to — worshiping him, trembling before him. True worship will shake us. We don't tremble in situations when we are comfortable. When things are going well, we don't tremble. Our palms sweat when we are nervous. We feel a pit in our stomach. I'm talking about the trembling of our hearts before the God who has made us, and before whom we will stand to be judged.

## The Lord Reigns and His Judgment Brings Joy — Verses 10-13

So why is it that the world needs to come before him? What will wake the world up to come to worship and to consider even just Jesus Christ? The fact that the Lord reigns. Verse 10: "Say among the nations: the Lord reigns! Yes, the world is established, it shall never be moved. He will judge the peoples with equity."

That ought to make us stand up straight and give attention to this God before whom we will stand. He reigns, and he will judge. You know, it is easy in our day to think of judgment only in negative terms. "Don't judge — you shouldn't be judging" — we hear that everywhere. It is the license for immorality. But do you understand that righteous judgment is a good thing? It is a wonderful thing.

I spent some time in Haiti, and there in one town, everything that was to be accomplished ran through one man. He controlled everything. This was no democracy, no republic — this was a dictatorship. And not a benevolent dictatorship. When a people live under oppressive judgment, the people suffer. But those people want justice in their world.

It wasn't difficult for our brothers and sisters in Micah's day to imagine such a desperate situation. We have an expression of condemnation on oppressors in Micah chapter 2: "Woe to those who devise wickedness and work evil on their beds. When the morning dawns, they perform it, because it is in the power of their hand. They covet fields and seize them, and houses and take them away." And the people in view in Micah's day are not just some selfish businessman — it is their governing officials. "The heads give judgment for a bribe, the priests teach for a price, the prophets practice divination for money."

This is the backdrop of a great promise — the promise of a redeemer. And listen to the prophecy in Micah 5:2: "But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah, who are too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient of days." He's coming to be a ruler because they know the oppression of their rulers. They cry out for a ruler who is righteous and just. And he will come through the little town of Bethlehem, and "he shall be great to the ends of the earth."

His judgment comes, and his judgment brings joy. Listen in verse 11: "Let the heavens be glad and let the earth rejoice. Let the sea roar and all that fills it. Let the field exalt and everything in it. Then shall all the trees of the forest sing for joy before the Lord, for he comes, for he comes to judge the earth. He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples in his faithfulness."

## Conclusion: A Call to Go

The Lord reigns. He reigns in all the earth. All the peoples of the earth need to hear of his salvation proclaimed from day to day. So sing to the Lord a new song, and sing to the Lord, all the earth — sing to the Lord, bless his name, and tell of his salvation from day to day.

It is a vision that God gives us here of joyful worship to the glorious one, the only one who deserves that true worship. And so brothers and sisters, it is a question of our devotion. We are called here to go. We are called to go and say among the nations, "The Lord reigns." We are called to go and declare his glory. There is action for us to take.

It is not just for us to gather together and enjoy and worship — though we should, we must. That is part of obedience to what we are called to here. But we are also part of expressing that glory to the ends of the earth.

May the Lord give us his mercy and his grace and his strength in that endeavor. Would you bow your heads with me. Gracious Lord, glorious Lord, we come to you with this congregation of people gathered to declare that you are glorious, to sing the praises of your name, to ascribe to you the glory that is due your name. And the families around the earth, Lord, may we be used for your glory, because that is what you deserve. May the worship that you receive from us and from those to whom we speak be pleasing to you. In Christ's name. Amen.
